source_cards.spark: Adaptive Resonance Theory — the learning architecture inside stigmergy's mesh — solved your exact tension decades ago: it holds a system open to new patterns while preventing old stable ones from being overwritten, using a single tunable parameter called vigilance. Your session governor needs to do the same thing with gap-chasing cycles.
source_cards.bridge: Your flow governor faces the stability-plasticity tension in a concrete form: a high-priority gap, if left unchecked, will pull the wandering wave toward it until the wave is no longer wandering — it is just gap-filling. ART's vigilance parameter is a direct mechanical analog to the injection budget your governor needs: it is the knob that says 'this gap-chasing bias is allowed up to this magnitude before the system refuses to update further.' The saturation-based convergence maps onto your 'convergence by saturation not exhaustion' node exactly — ART stops updating a category not because a timer expired but because the category is genuinely full, which is the signal your governor needs to recognize that a gap has been adequately pursued rather than merely aged out. Complement coding maps onto your goal-neutralization requirement: instead of a policy rule that says 'don't over-allocate to gap X,' it is a structural guarantee baked into the routing mesh itself — a gap that has consumed enough agent capacity raises its own routing cost, crowding itself out automatically.
source_cards.source_shape: ART networks route incoming signals through a competitive mesh where workers bid for recognition; the first worker whose familiarity with the signal clears a threshold wins (satisficing, not exhaustive search). The vigilance parameter controls how strict that threshold is — low vigilance lets a single category absorb many dissimilar signals, high vigilance forces new categories to form rather than stretching old ones. A mechanism called complement coding prevents any one worker from monopolizing routing capacity: full workers raise the effective vigilance threshold against themselves, structurally guaranteeing that no single pattern consumes all available bandwidth.
source_cards.use: Consider implementing your injection budget not as a policy cap ('gap X may claim at most N agents') but as a vigilance-gated feedback loop: as a gap accumulates routed attempts, it raises a threshold that incoming dispatch signals must clear to keep routing toward it — so the gap self-limits by saturation rather than by a governor decision that has to be made explicitly each cycle.
source_cards.limit: Stigmergy has no attempt-history discriminator: it cannot tell the difference between a gap that is empty because nothing exists to fill it and a gap that is empty because routing went wrong. Stale patterns simply decay by age — that is exhaustion-based halting, not saturation-based, and it means the architecture cannot expose the 'productive ignorance' your system needs to surface. More critically, stigmergy's mesh is uniformly goal-blind: there is no second detector that knows what the draft needs, only signal statistics. The contamination-prevention architecture your anchor requires — a goal-blind structural finder running clean alongside a goal-aware auditor — has no counterpart here. The bridge holds for the stability-plasticity mechanism and the self-crowding guarantee, but it does not touch the dual-detector fusion problem at all.

source_cards.spark: Software circuit breakers — designed to stop hammering a broken external service — turn out to share an exact structural shape with the problem of knowing when to stop chasing an unfillable gap: both are mechanisms that convert a pattern of repeated failure into a halting signal, rather than waiting for global exhaustion.
source_cards.bridge: Your orchestrator faces the same shape at the gap level. Each open gap in the session is its own 'dependency': the orchestrator keeps dispatching agents toward it, and each failed fill attempt is a failure event against that specific gap. Right now, the only signal available is global diminishing returns — the session slowing down as a whole — which cannot distinguish 'this gap is genuinely empty, no evidence exists' from 'this gap is under-attempted, just keep looking.' A per-gap circuit breaker would attach a failure counter directly to each gap node, and when that counter crosses a threshold, it trips: the orchestrator stops allocating resources to that gap, not because the session is tired, but because that specific gap has accumulated enough successive failures to warrant a different classification. The attempt history that was previously just a log becomes the discriminator — the gap moves from 'unlooked' to 'likely empty' based on its own record, not the session's.
source_cards.source_shape: A circuit breaker in systems like forge-os-core sits in front of a dependency — a database, an API, a memory backend — and counts consecutive failures against it. When failures cross a threshold, the breaker trips: the system stops sending calls to that dependency and routes around it, rather than piling up timeouts. The key move is that the decision to stop is made per-dependency, based on that dependency's own failure history, not on some global sense that 'things are going badly overall.'
source_cards.use: Consider adding a per-gap attempt counter to each node in the noticeboard's gap registry, and defining a trip threshold — when a gap accumulates N consecutive failed fills, the orchestrator reclassifies it as 'saturation-halted' and stops dispatching toward it, freeing that allocation for remaining open gaps.
source_cards.limit: forge-os-core's circuit breaker was built to protect against unhealthy external services, not to reason about why a gap is empty. It has no mechanism for distinguishing 'the gap is empty because no evidence exists in the corpus' from 'the gap persists because the search strategy is wrong and a different approach would fill it' — those two failure modes look identical to a pure failure counter. Correctly classifying a tripped gap requires a higher-order evaluator that reads the attempt metadata and infers failure semantics: did the agents look in the right places and find nothing, or did they look in the wrong places repeatedly? That evaluator is not in the source and would need to be built on top of the circuit breaker primitive for the bridge to hold all the way to convergence by saturation.

source_cards.spark: A single-agent runtime detects when it is stuck not by counting steps but by recognizing that it keeps making the same move — and that pattern-recognition primitive is exactly what the anchor's gap-type discriminator needs to distinguish a gap that hasn't been tried hard enough from a gap that genuinely cannot be filled.
source_cards.bridge: Your flow governor already watches findings arrive and needs to decide what kind of silence it is seeing — is a gap quiet because no agent has reached it yet, or because every agent that reached it came back empty? OmniCoreAgent's signature tracking answers that question at the tool-call level by the same logic: identical repeated signatures mean saturation, not under-attempt. The mapping is direct: replace 'tool-call signature' with 'attempt fingerprint per gap node' and the discriminator is the same mechanism. Saturation — the same gap visited with the same approach, repeatedly, with no new finding — is the signal for 'unfillable'; sparse or varied attempts with no finding is the signal for 'under-attempted'. The harness's design also instantiates flow sovereignty with quality delegation: the runtime owns the loop, but something outside it owns the verdict on whether the result is good enough — which mirrors your governor holding flow control while deferring structural quality judgment to the cycle layer above it.
source_cards.source_shape: OmniCoreAgent tracks every tool call by hashing its signature — the function name plus its arguments — into a SHA256 fingerprint. When the same fingerprint reappears, the harness recognizes a stuck loop and halts, not because a step counter expired but because the call pattern saturated: the agent ran out of new moves. The harness also reserves final judgment for a human, keeping autonomous execution sovereign over the loop while delegating quality assessment outward.
source_cards.use: Consider implementing attempt fingerprinting at the gap level in your governor — hash the (gap_id, agent_id, approach_vector) tuple for each attempt, accumulate these per gap, and treat a high repeat-rate on identical fingerprints as a saturation signal that promotes the gap to 'unfillable' rather than re-queuing it for another wave.
source_cards.limit: OmniCoreAgent is a single-agent harness with no concept of a shared structural skeleton, no multi-agent wave coordination, and no recursive self-amendment loop. Its loop detection operates entirely at the tool-call level — it knows when one agent is spinning, not when a knowledge structure has a gap topology that multiple agents are collectively failing to close. Critically, it does not distinguish 'unfillable' from 'under-attempted' in any structural sense; it only detects repetition within one agent's own call history. Generalizing this to your multi-agent, shared-noticeboard architecture requires building the cross-agent attempt aggregation layer from scratch — the primitive is here, but the infrastructure that makes it meaningful across waves is not.

selection.why_these_cards: ART's vigilance/complement-coding gives a self-saturating threshold; the circuit breaker gives a per-gap failure counter; OmniCoreAgent's signature-hashing gives a way to read WHETHER repeated attempts were actually different. Three different domains (neural learning, fault tolerance, agent loop detection) all touching the same nerve: when does accumulated attempt-pressure mean STOP versus RE-ROUTE.
selection.spark: A circuit breaker trips on consecutive failures but cannot tell 'looked wrong' from 'genuinely empty.' ART raises its own threshold as a category fills. OmniCoreAgent distinguishes saturation from spinning by hashing the move signature. Fuse all three and the breaker becomes diagnostic, not just protective.
selection.motive: Directly answers question (3): distinguishing unfillable (terminate) from under-attempted (re-route deeper) from OBSERVABLE signals, with no quality judge.
selection.tension: Circuit breaker counts failures blindly and would terminate a gap that was merely approached identically ten times; OmniCoreAgent says identical-signature repetition is exactly the OPPOSITE signal (it means try a NEW approach, not give up). The breaker says 'N failures = dead'; the fingerprint says 'N IDENTICAL failures = your strategy is dead, the gap may be alive.' That contradiction is the productive friction.
selection.discovery_path: Circuit breaker asserts: N consecutive failures → trip and abandon. OmniCoreAgent asserts: N identical-signature repeats → halt because the AGENT is stuck, not the territory. These cannot both be the abandonment rule unless we separate the axis being measured. Separating them forces a 2-D classifier: failure-COUNT on one axis, approach-DIVERSITY (signature spread) on the other. ART supplies the saturation threshold that closes the loop: a gap raises its OWN re-route cost as approach-diversity is exhausted. The thesis is the answer to: what trips first, abandonment or redirection?

blends.thesis: A 'diversity-gated circuit breaker' that trips on TWO orthogonal axes read from attempt fingerprints: high failure-count + high approach-diversity → terminate (unfillable, every angle tried); high failure-count + low approach-diversity → re-route deeper with a forced-novel approach vector (under-attempted, agents are spinning). The gap classifies ITSELF from the entropy of its own attempt signatures, no quality judge required.
blends.mechanism: Each attempt writes a (gap_id, approach_vector) fingerprint to the gap node. The governor computes two scalars per gap: failure count and Shannon entropy of the approach-vector distribution. A breaker matrix maps the quadrant: low-entropy/high-fail → inject a constraint forcing the next wave to use an unused approach class (ART-style: the exhausted approach raises its own routing cost, crowding itself out); high-entropy/high-fail → reclassify saturation-halted; low-fail → leave open. Termination requires diversity exhaustion, not mere repetition.
blends.emergent_structure: Approach-DIVERSITY as the discriminating second axis. Neither the circuit breaker (failure count only) nor ART (similarity-to-existing-category only) measures the spread of HOW a gap was attacked. The blend makes attempt-entropy a first-class termination signal — a gap is unfillable only when its attempt-signature entropy is saturated, not when its failure count is high.
blends.advances_cushion: Gives the orchestrator an observable, judge-free rule for the terminate/re-route fork — the single hardest discrimination in the cushion question. Re-route is no longer 'try again' but 'try a structurally-forced-different angle,' which is precisely what under-attempted gaps need.

source_cards.spark: A production orchestration engine evolves its own topology by mutating, testing, and rolling back — the same shape as a flow governor that needs to temporarily warp its framing to chase a gap without permanently bending its structure.
source_cards.bridge: Your flow governor needs to chase coverage gaps by temporarily biasing how it frames and routes incoming findings — but it cannot afford to let that bias calcify into a permanent distortion of the session's structure. forge-os-core's snapshot-rollback cycle is exactly that mechanism in concrete form: the snapshot is the governor's current framing state, the mutation is the temporary bias injection toward an under-covered region, and the rollback is the restoration of neutral framing once the gap is filled or the bias proves unproductive. The circuit breaker maps to the halt signal your governor needs when a framing distortion persists across multiple cycles without closing the gap — a sign that the gap may be structurally unreachable from the current approach, not merely under-attempted. The key correspondence is that commit/rollback makes the distortion safe to attempt: the governor can lean hard into a gap precisely because it knows the lean is reversible.
source_cards.source_shape: forge-os-core runs an evolution loop: Observe → Hypothesize → Mutate → Evaluate → Commit/Rollback. At each cycle the engine takes a snapshot of its current topology, applies a mutation (a biased sampling strategy, a rewired connection, a changed weight), then evaluates whether the mutation improved outcomes. If it did, the change commits; if not, the snapshot is restored. A circuit breaker watches for repeated failures — if mutations keep reverting, the loop suspends entirely rather than thrashing. The result is a system that can explore structural changes aggressively because every change is bounded and reversible, and the breaker prevents runaway distortion from accumulating.
source_cards.use: Consider implementing the governor's bias injection as an explicit snapshot-and-rollback cycle rather than a continuous parameter drift — each gap-chase attempt starts by checkpointing the current framing state, runs for a bounded window, then either commits the new framing or restores the checkpoint, with a circuit breaker that flags gaps that have triggered N consecutive rollbacks as candidates for a different detection strategy.
source_cards.limit: forge-os-core's evolution loop is entirely fitness-driven: every mutation is evaluated against a goal-aware signal, so the system only ever asks 'did this improve the outcome?' — it has no mechanism for goal-blind structural discovery, and no concept of productive ignorance exposure (deliberately surfacing what it doesn't know it doesn't know). Your governor's dual-signal architecture — fusing a goal-blind gap finder with a goal-aware one without letting either contaminate the other — has no analog here. More critically, the epistemic halting problem your anchor faces (distinguishing a genuinely empty gap from a gap that simply hasn't been attempted enough) is not touched by forge-os-core's completeness check, which only counts coverage, not the quality or depth of what covered it.

source_cards.spark: A tool orchestration system that expands or shrinks a tool's freedom based on its delivery track record turns out to be a working physical model of the 'bounded reversible bias' your gap-chasing agents need — autonomy that earns itself and retracts automatically on failure.
source_cards.bridge: Your flow governor has the same structural need: bias injected into a gap-chasing agent must be bounded so it cannot permanently distort the session's structural model, and reversible so a single failed excursion snaps the agent back rather than leaving drift baked in. organism-core's lifecycle states map directly onto that requirement — 'earned autonomy' is the grant phase, 'track record validation' is the boundary condition, and 'demotion on failure' is the reversal mechanism. Where organism-core asks 'did this tool meet its DoD?', your governor would ask 'did this agent's gap-chase return findings that integrated cleanly into the skeleton?' — and the answer drives the same expand-or-retract logic. The correspondence holds at the mechanism level: a gap-chasing agent that has found useful things before is allowed to wander farther; one whose last excursion produced noise gets its radius yanked back immediately.
source_cards.source_shape: organism-core runs tools through a lifecycle state machine: a tool starts constrained, earns wider autonomy as its track record of meeting success criteria grows, and gets demoted — autonomy revoked — the moment it drifts. The mechanism is purely behavioral: the system watches what a tool actually delivers, not what it promises, and the state machine is the enforcement layer that makes expansion and contraction automatic rather than human-managed. Freedom is never granted in advance; it is always a trailing function of demonstrated reliability.
source_cards.use: Consider building a per-agent attempt profile — a lightweight track record that the flow governor consults before authorizing each gap-chase — so that drift radius is not a fixed parameter but a live function of that agent's integration history across the current session.
source_cards.limit: organism-core's autonomy expansion is purely performance-based against a fixed, pre-specified success criterion — it never touches or distorts its own structural model, because it has none. Your bias problem is fundamentally epistemic: a gap-chasing agent temporarily reframes the whole skeleton to pursue a gap, and the risk is that the reframing outlasts the excursion and permanently misrepresents the structure. organism-core has no skeleton, no self-model, and no recursive reassembly loop — it is a linear orchestrator with a fixed truth store. Its attempt-history discriminates tool trust, not gap type, so it gives you no guidance on how to classify a bias as 'fertile' or 'barren' relative to a structural gap, only on whether a tool delivered against a criterion you already knew how to specify.

selection.why_these_cards: forge-os-core gives snapshot/mutate/rollback so a framing bias is REVERSIBLE; ART's vigilance gives a budget that self-saturates rather than being capped by policy; organism-core's earned-autonomy lifecycle gives a track-record-driven radius. All three bear on biasing re-exploration toward gaps WITHOUT permanently deforming the structure that made the first pass diverse.
selection.spark: Bias injection toward a gap risks collapsing diversity. Snapshot/rollback makes the lean reversible; ART makes a single gap crowd ITSELF out as it consumes capacity; earned-autonomy ties the lean's radius to the agent's integration track record. Combine: a bias that is reversible, self-throttling, AND merit-weighted.
selection.motive: Answers question (4): bias re-exploration toward identified gaps WITHOUT collapsing the diversity of the first pass.
selection.tension: forge-os-core commits a mutation if fitness improves — a goal-AWARE, convergent pressure that, run alone, would pull the whole wave toward the highest-priority gap and kill divergence. ART's complement-coding pushes the OPPOSITE way: a gap that consumes too much capacity automatically raises its own cost, defending diversity. One commits to what works; the other refuses to let any single winner monopolize. That tug is the engine.
selection.discovery_path: forge-os-core asserts: bias is safe because rollback makes it reversible — but reversibility says nothing about MAGNITUDE while active. ART asserts: a category cannot monopolize bandwidth because filling it raises its own threshold. Reversibility (forge) and self-throttling (ART) cannot both be the diversity guarantee unless they govern different phases. Mapping forces the question: what bounds the bias DURING the excursion, before rollback? organism-core answers: the radius is a trailing function of demonstrated integration. The thesis fuses temporal (rollback), capacity (vigilance), and merit (track record) bounds into one bias-injection contract.

blends.thesis: A 'self-defeating bias injection': each gap-chase is a reversible snapshot whose injected bias magnitude DECAYS as the gap consumes wave capacity (ART crowding) and whose maximum radius is gated by the chasing agent's integration track record (earned autonomy). Diversity is preserved not by a policy cap but because every successful lean toward a gap structurally raises that gap's own future routing cost — productive gaps satisfy themselves out of dominance.
blends.mechanism: Governor checkpoints framing state, grants a gap-chase a bias budget = f(agent_track_record). As the gap accumulates routed agents, its effective routing threshold rises (complement coding), so each additional dispatch toward it must clear a higher bar — diverting the next agents elsewhere automatically. If the excursion fails to integrate cleanly, rollback restores neutral framing AND demotes the agent's radius. Diversity is the equilibrium of three competing pressures, not an enforced constraint.
blends.emergent_structure: Bias magnitude as a DECREASING function of its own success. Neither forge-os-core (bias persists until rollback) nor organism-core (autonomy grows with success) has a mechanism where pursuing a gap REDUCES future pull toward it. The blend makes diversity self-restoring: the more a gap is fed, the cheaper every OTHER gap becomes.
blends.advances_cushion: Solves the diversity-collapse half of the cushion without a separate diversity-policing module — the anti-collapse behavior falls out of the allocation mechanics themselves, which is exactly the judge-free, self-governing property the cushion demands.

source_cards.spark: A rules engine that resolves policy conflicts by assigning fixed precedence tiers — Deny beats Require beats Suggest — turns out to be a structural cousin of your dual-signal fusion problem: two gap detectors with different epistemic vantage points need to produce one unified dispatch without letting either detector's bias colonize the other's output.
source_cards.bridge: Your session-level flow governor sits between two gap detectors that see the noticeboard differently: one is goal-aware, reading gaps in relation to what the session is trying to accomplish; the other is goal-blind, reading gaps on structural grounds alone. These are not just different opinions — they are epistemically asymmetric, meaning one cannot simply average or interleave them without letting the stronger signal drown the quieter one. The forge-rules lattice names this problem precisely: when two rule sources overlap on the same scope, you need a tie-breaker that is fixed and transparent, not emergent. Mapping across: each detector is a 'rule tier,' the gaps they both flag are 'scope overlaps,' and the dispatch signal your governor emits is the 'resolved output.' The lattice insight is that you can pre-decide which detector wins in which class of conflict — goal-aware wins when the session has a live convergence signal, goal-blind wins when the session is still in divergent exploration — rather than trying to dynamically negotiate every collision.
source_cards.source_shape: The forge-rules engine lets authors write policy rules across many files, and at compile time it detects wherever two rules claim jurisdiction over the same scope. When a conflict is found, a fixed precedence lattice settles it: Deny always beats Require, Require always beats Suggest, no case-by-case negotiation. The lattice is decided once, at design time, so at runtime the engine never hesitates — it already knows which voice wins.
source_cards.use: Consider defining a small, explicit precedence table for your governor: enumerate the conflict classes (both detectors flag the same gap, detectors flag adjacent gaps, detectors disagree on gap priority) and assign a winner for each class at design time. This gives you a deterministic fusion path you can inspect and revise without touching the detectors themselves.
source_cards.limit: The forge-rules lattice works because its rules are static and pre-authored — the precedence hierarchy is fixed at compile time and never needs to revise itself. Your two gap detectors are self-amending: their evaluations shift as the session accumulates findings, which means any precedence assignment you make today may be wrong tomorrow as the detectors' outputs drift. Worse, forge-rules detects scope overlap syntactically, using file glob patterns — your governor would need to assess whether two gap recommendations are semantically or structurally proximate, a judgment that pattern matching cannot make. The lattice shape holds as a design metaphor, but the actual mechanism requires a dynamic, context-sensitive definition of 'specificity' that the source does not provide.

source_cards.spark: A system built for founder-engineers invented an organ whose entire job is to watch the system's own reasoning for self-deception — goal-blind enough to catch motivated thinking, goal-aware enough to know which biases matter. Your flow governor needs exactly that dual posture, and organism named it as a distinct architectural role.
source_cards.bridge: Your flow governor already handles dual-signal fusion — integrating convergence pressure and divergence pressure without letting either contaminate the other's signal. Eyes maps onto the contamination-prevention side of that problem at one level up: it is the mechanism that keeps the orchestrator's framing from quietly drifting toward whatever the system has already invested in, which is exactly the distortion your 'temporary framing distortion' node is supposed to introduce deliberately and then re-integrate cleanly. The pitfall layer's override-history discrimination maps onto your 'attempt-history as gap-type discriminator': the mechanism for telling a genuine structural gap from an avoidance gap is the same in both systems — you read the pattern of what was skipped or overridden before, not just what is absent now. The structural correspondence holds because both systems are trying to solve the same underlying problem: how does a cycle that is deep inside its own reasoning notice that its framing has bent, and how does it tell motivated drift from legitimate narrowing?
source_cards.source_shape: Organism is a nine-organ coordination protocol where each organ has a fixed role in a 7-step task flow, with tiered visibility into what other organs see. One organ, Eyes, is specifically chartered to surface cognitive biases, environmental drift, and self-deception — not to the system, but to the human founder who triggers and steers every cycle. A separate 100-pitfall layer fires Block, Warn, or Note signals drawn from the founder's own override history, distinguishing 'this gap exists because the pattern is absent' from 'this gap exists because the founder has been avoiding it' — a discrimination that is only possible by reading the history of prior gate overrides.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your flow governor needs an Eyes-equivalent — a lightweight internal probe that runs not against the incoming findings but against the orchestrator's own recent framing decisions, specifically looking for the signature of motivated convergence: gates that keep firing in the same direction, leads that keep getting deprioritized by the same mechanism. You could seed it from the attempt-history log the governor already maintains.
source_cards.limit: Organism's loop is neither autonomous nor self-amending: a human founder triggers every cycle, confirms or overrides every gate, and is the entity Eyes reports drift to. There is no mechanism by which organism detects framing drift and re-integrates it internally — Eyes flags the problem upward, it does not close the loop itself. Your anchor's halting criterion is structural completeness the system evaluates on its own; organism's convergence signal is the founder deciding to ship. The bridge holds for the shape of bias-detection-as-distinct-architectural-role and override-history-as-gap-discriminator, but it breaks entirely at autonomy, self-amendment, and system-evaluated halting — those three properties have no analog in organism at all.

source_cards.spark: A human-AI collaboration toolkit solves the same 'two detectors, one signal' problem your flow governor faces — by deliberately feeding different representations of the same state to each cognition, so neither contaminates the other before they fuse.
source_cards.bridge: Your flow governor runs two detectors against the same skeleton state: a goal-aware auditor that knows what the session is trying to reach, and a goal-blind structural finder that surfaces resonance without being steered by intent. The risk is cross-contamination — if both detectors consume the same representation of the board, the goal-aware signal leaks into the structural finder and you no longer have two independent detectors, you have one detector running twice. The digital-stoic documentation split is a direct instantiation of this problem's solution: the same underlying state is translated into two different representations before it reaches each cognition, preserving epistemic independence until the fusion step. The divergence-convergence tension maps cleanly too — your auditor is the convergence arm (pulling findings toward the session's goal), your structural finder is the divergence arm (wandering without that pull), and the governor's job is to hold both arms open long enough before collapsing them into a judgment.
source_cards.source_shape: The digital-stoic-org toolkit is built around a strict documentation split: human-facing docs are scannable and link-heavy, designed for progressive orientation; LLM-facing docs are self-contained and token-dense, designed for cold-context invocation. The two cognitions — human and AI — are never handed the same artifact, because they process differently and conflating their inputs would degrade both. The architecture calls this 'mutual sharpening': the human supplies convergent pressure (intent, phase gates, verification), the AI supplies divergent pressure (blind-spot challenges, assumption surfacing), and the loop compounds precisely because neither mode collapses into the other.
source_cards.use: Consider whether your two detectors currently receive identical board representations — if they do, design a translation layer that strips goal-context from the input the structural finder sees, mirroring the toolkit's documentation split, so independence is structural rather than merely intended.
source_cards.limit: The digital-stoic system is not autonomous — it is architecturally dependent on a human at every phase gate to set intent, select workflow phase, and decide when the loop is done. Your governor owns its own halting decision: it must distinguish a gap that is genuinely unfillable from one that is merely under-attempted, using attempt history as its evidence. The human-steered system has no equivalent of this problem because the human simply decides to stop; the convergence mechanism is a person, not a criterion. Any mapping that treats the toolkit's phase gates as analogs to your governor's halting logic will import a hidden assumption — that a human is available to absorb the hard cases — which your architecture explicitly does not have.

selection.why_these_cards: forge-rules gives a fixed precedence lattice for resolving two overlapping signals deterministically; Eyes/pitfall-layer gives override-history as a way to detect motivated drift; digital-stoic gives the representation-split that keeps two cognitions epistemically independent. All three are about fusing two asymmetric signals into ONE allocation priority without one colonizing the other.
selection.spark: A static precedence lattice (forge-rules) resolves conflicts deterministically but goes stale as detectors drift. Eyes reads override-history to catch when the SAME bias keeps winning. Fuse them: a lattice whose precedence is conditioned on, and audited by, the history of which detector keeps winning — so motivated convergence trips an alarm.
selection.motive: Answers question (1): fuse multiple independent gap/uncertainty signals into a single allocation priority — and does it without letting the stronger (goal-aware) signal drown the quieter (goal-blind) one.
selection.tension: forge-rules insists precedence is fixed at design time — never negotiated, never revised; that is its whole virtue. Eyes insists the opposite: you must READ the running history of which voice wins to detect drift, because a fixed rule that keeps firing one way IS the signature of motivated convergence. A frozen lattice cannot see its own pathology; Eyes exists precisely to see it. They cannot both be right about whether the tie-break should adapt.
selection.discovery_path: forge-rules asserts: deterministic fusion requires a precedence decided once and never revised. Eyes asserts: the danger is a gate that keeps firing the same direction — a fixed bias is detectable only by watching the history of wins. If precedence is frozen (forge) yet a frozen tie-break is the exact thing Eyes is built to flag, then the lattice must be deterministic at decision-time but AUDITED at meta-time. The digital-stoic split resolves how: keep the goal-blind detector's INPUT representation goal-stripped so its votes stay independent, making the win-history a clean signal rather than a contaminated one. Thesis answers: how can fusion be both deterministic AND drift-resistant?

blends.thesis: A 'two-tier fusion lattice with a drift auditor': fusion is deterministic per-cycle via a fixed precedence table (goal-aware wins under live convergence signal, goal-blind wins during divergence), BUT a meta-layer logs the WIN-HISTORY of the lattice and trips when the same detector wins beyond an expected rate — the signature of motivated convergence — forcing a temporary precedence inversion. The goal-blind detector's input is goal-stripped (digital-stoic split) so its votes remain an uncontaminated baseline against which drift is measured.
blends.mechanism: Each cycle the lattice resolves the two detectors' gap rankings into one priority deterministically. A win-ledger records which detector prevailed per conflict class. A meta-monitor compares observed win-rate against the rate implied by the current orchestration mode; an anomalous streak (goal-aware winning during what should be divergence) trips a forced inversion for K cycles, re-opening diversity. Because the goal-blind detector never sees goal-context, its ranking is a fixed reference frame the auditor can trust.
blends.emergent_structure: A win-RATE expectation tied to orchestration mode. Neither forge-rules (no history at all) nor Eyes (history of human overrides, not of an automated tie-break) has a self-monitoring lattice that knows how often each side SHOULD win in a given mode and flags deviation. The blend makes the fusion rule's own voting record an input to the next fusion.
blends.advances_cushion: Gives a concrete, inspectable answer to signal-fusion (q1) that simultaneously protects against the cushion's named failure mode — motivated convergence collapsing diversity — with no human referee in the loop.

source_cards.spark: A 3-billion-parameter model hits a hard capacity ceiling and stops — not because it ran out of things to try, but because the bounded system filled up. That's a different kind of halt than exhaustion, and it maps directly onto the diminishing-returns stop signal your flow governor is trying to produce.
source_cards.bridge: Your flow governor watches findings arrive and has to decide when the session has converged — when to close rather than keep pulling. The 3B ceiling maps onto the governor's representational budget: the set of gap-types the session can still usefully distinguish. When that set is saturated — when new findings are landing in slots that are already filled, producing no new gap-type discrimination — the system has hit its architectural limit, not its logical one. The 'convergence by saturation not exhaustion' node is exactly this: the governor doesn't need to prove it has seen everything, it needs to detect that additional input is no longer producing new structure. The capacity ceiling, reframed, becomes a live diagnostic — the governor watches not just whether gaps are closing, but whether the incoming signal is still capable of opening new ones.
source_cards.source_shape: The Thousand Token Wood project ran a multi-agent economy inside a model with a fixed parameter count — 3B, no more. Coordination had to emerge within that ceiling, not beyond it. The system's halting criterion wasn't 'we checked everything'; it was 'the capacity available to represent new distinctions is saturated — there is no room left for a meaningfully different answer.' The ceiling is architectural, not logical, and saturation is the signal that the work is done.
source_cards.use: Consider instrumenting the governor to track 'gap-type novelty rate' as a first-class signal alongside gap-closure rate — when new findings stop introducing gap-types that haven't appeared before, that's a saturation signal, and it could trigger the diminishing-returns halt independently of whether all existing gaps are resolved.
source_cards.limit: The article describes the emergent output of a capacity-bounded system but says nothing about how the system diagnosed its own incompleteness mid-run, or how it distinguished a gap that was genuinely unfillable from one that was merely under-attempted. Those two discriminations — dual gap-detector fusion and attempt-history as gap-type discriminator — are the load-bearing mechanisms in your anchor and have no correspondent here. The bridge holds for the halting-criterion shape only; it breaks entirely at the question of how the system knows, in the moment, which kind of gap it is looking at.

source_cards.spark: A system that researches its own definition of done before it acts — and revises that definition after — turns quality-gating into a recursive self-amendment loop, which is exactly the shape the anchor's convergence-by-saturation mechanism is trying to achieve.
source_cards.bridge: The anchor's attempt-history-as-gap-discriminator maps directly onto organism-core's lifecycle-stage evolution: both use what has already been tried to decide what kind of gap remains and how to address it next. The anchor's bounded reversible bias injection — nudging the search without locking it in — corresponds to organism-core's DoD research phase, which introduces a scoped, revisable success frame before each action cycle rather than hardcoding one at initialization. The anchor's convergence-by-saturation criterion maps cleanly onto organism-core's score-gated halt: neither system stops because it ran out of options, both stop because the accumulated evidence crosses a structural completeness threshold. The resonance is real and load-bearing across these three mechanisms — organism-core is essentially a working proof that a DoD-driven recursive loop can replace exhaustion-based termination with saturation-based termination.
source_cards.source_shape: organism-core is an AI orchestration architecture that, before executing any task, first researches what 'done' actually means for that task — producing a Definition of Done (DoD) from external sources rather than assuming one upfront. After execution, it scores results against that DoD and feeds the score back into the next cycle's lifecycle stage, so the system's own history of attempts determines how it behaves next. Halt is not triggered by exhaustion of tries but by saturation: the system stops when the DoD score crosses a threshold, meaning it stops when it knows it has succeeded, not when it runs out of moves.
source_cards.use: Consider whether the anchor's convergence logic could adopt organism-core's pattern of researching the DoD fresh each cycle rather than inheriting it from session initialization — this would let the halt criterion itself evolve as the assembly skeleton fills in, without requiring the goal-blind and goal-aware signals to merge prematurely.
source_cards.limit: The anchor's architecture requires keeping a goal-blind structural finder and a goal-aware auditor strictly separated so neither contaminates the other's signal — organism-core collapses these into a single DoD engine that is goal-aware from the first step, so the dual-signal tension the anchor demands is simply absent here, not solved differently. Additionally, organism-core allocates resources by lifecycle stage and parallel tool execution, not by reading the topological shape of gaps in an assembly skeleton — so the anchor's structural-topology-driven prioritization has no counterpart in this source and cannot be borrowed from it.

source_cards.spark: A hackathon project built three completely different-feeling AI tools from a single four-part runtime skeleton — and that skeleton's conductor logic maps cleanly onto your cycle orchestrator's job of scheduling agents and knowing when to stop.
source_cards.bridge: Your cycle orchestrator does the same two-track job as the conductor: it reacts to findings arriving on the noticeboard (subscriptions) and also drives the loop forward on its own cadence (ticks). The ledger-as-single-source-of-truth maps directly onto your noticeboard as the shared substrate — if every agent output is an appended event rather than a mutation, then the 'flow sovereignty with quality delegation' property you want falls out naturally: the loop runs uninterrupted, and a human reviewer can reconstruct any intermediate state as a projection after the fact without having touched anything during the run. The conductor's diminishing-returns halt signal is structurally the same as your halt signal: both watch the rate of meaningful new output and cut the loop when that rate drops below a threshold. The mapping holds tightest at the scheduling and halting layer — those three components (conductor, ledger, governor) are doing recognizably the same work as your orchestrator, noticeboard, and halt signal.
source_cards.source_shape: The source architecture has four parts: an append-only ledger that records every event as the single source of truth, a conductor that runs two scheduling tracks simultaneously (subscriptions that fire when specific events arrive, and ticks that fire on a clock), stateless agents that read from the ledger and emit typed events, and projections that reconstruct any view of the system by folding over the event history. The conductor also enforces a diminishing-returns governor — once new events stop changing the output meaningfully, it halts the loop. Nothing is mutated in place; every piece of state is a pure read over the log.
source_cards.use: Consider implementing your noticeboard as a strict append-only event log and your cycle checkpoints as pure projection functions over that log — this would make your structural completeness check a stateless fold rather than a live inspection, which is easier to test, replay, and hand to a human reviewer without disrupting the running loop.
source_cards.limit: The source has no concept of structural gap detection, no auditor, and no mechanism for ranking what is missing versus what is merely unconfirmed. Its agents emit events based on persona and context, but they are reacting — none of them are building toward a skeleton of required coverage or distinguishing between a gap that blocks completion and one that does not. The anchor's core problem — fusing goal-blind discovery signals with goal-aware gap signals, and managing the distortion that temporary framing creates — has no analogue anywhere in the source's reaction-tick loop. The bridge holds at the scheduling and halting layer; it breaks entirely at the question of completeness, gap-type discrimination, and dual-signal fusion.

selection.why_these_cards: The 3B capacity-ceiling card gives saturation-as-architectural-limit (gap-type NOVELTY rate); organism-core gives a Definition-of-Done re-derived each cycle (a moving completeness target); the ledger/conductor card gives a diminishing-returns governor reading the rate of meaningful new output. Three takes on STOP, in tension over whether 'done' is fixed, discovered, or capacity-bound.
selection.spark: Three stop signals exist: capacity-saturation (no room for a new distinction), DoD-satisfaction (a re-derived target is met), and output-rate decay (new events stop changing the projection). None alone is safe — capacity can saturate prematurely, a DoD can be under-specified, rate-decay can be a lull. Braid them into one stop decision with three independent vetoes.
selection.motive: Answers question (5): what governs the overall STOP — coverage-complete vs diminishing-returns vs hard-cap — with no quality judge in the loop.
selection.tension: organism-core re-derives 'done' each cycle, so the target MOVES — completeness is relative to a freshly-researched DoD. The capacity-ceiling card says 'done' is ABSOLUTE and architectural — saturation when no new gap-type can be represented, independent of any goal. A moving target and a fixed ceiling cannot both be the stop criterion unless they constrain different failure modes of stopping. The conductor's rate-decay is a third, purely temporal signal that can fire while BOTH others are still live.
selection.discovery_path: organism-core asserts: stop when a re-derived DoD score crosses threshold (coverage-complete, goal-relative). Capacity-ceiling card asserts: stop when gap-type novelty rate hits zero (diminishing-returns, goal-blind). Conductor asserts: stop when output stops changing the projection (rate, time-relative). Three criteria, three different things being measured — they will disagree. If they can disagree, then using any one alone risks the failure the other two would catch: a satisfied DoD over a half-explored space, a novelty-floor that's really a lull, a rate-stall mid-discovery. The resolution is not to pick one but to require AGREEMENT, with a hard cap as the only unilateral stop. Thesis answers: how to combine three asymmetric stop signals safely without a quality judge?

blends.thesis: A 'triangulated halt' where STOP requires consensus across three orthogonal, judge-free signals — goal-blind gap-type novelty rate (capacity-saturation), goal-aware re-derived DoD score (coverage), and time-relative output-change rate (diminishing-returns) — and any single signal can VETO a stop but none can force one, except a hard cap which alone fires unilaterally. The three signals are deliberately measured in different reference frames so they fail independently.
blends.mechanism: Each cycle computes three scalars: novelty-rate (new gap-types per wave), DoD-satisfaction (against a freshly re-derived target), and change-rate (delta in the projection fold over the ledger). A halt proposal requires all three below their thresholds simultaneously; if any is still high, the loop continues (veto). A hard agent/compute cap overrides as a safety floor. Because the goal-blind novelty signal can keep the loop alive even when the goal-aware DoD is satisfied, premature goal-relative closure is structurally prevented.
blends.emergent_structure: Reference-frame INDEPENDENCE as a halting safeguard. No source combines stop-signals chosen specifically because they fail in uncorrelated ways. The blend makes the safety of the stop decision come from the orthogonality of its inputs — a single satisfied criterion is never trusted, and the goal-blind signal can outvote goal-aware satisfaction, which is exactly the contamination-resistance the architecture needs.
blends.advances_cushion: Directly resolves the cushion's hardest stated tension — coverage-complete VS diminishing-returns VS hard-cap — by refusing to choose, and instead making them mutual checks, all computable without a quality judge in the loop.
